questions.prompt: Permanent debt of $200M at 6% interest. Corporate tax rate 25%. What is the PV of the interest tax shield? Answer in $M.
questions.explanation: PV(TS) = t × D = 0.25 × 200 = $50M. The tax-shield perpetuity reduces to t·D when discounted at r_D.

questions.prompt: MM with corporate taxes says firm value as a function of debt is:
choices: V_L = V_U + t·D (rises linearly with debt)
questions.explanation: V_L = V_U + t·D. More debt = more tax shield = more value, before distress costs kick in.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't WACC stay constant when taxes are introduced (compared to MM without taxes)?
choices: Because the after-tax debt rate (1-t)·r_D is lower than r_D, so the weighted average tips toward the cheaper debt component as leverage rises
Because cost of debt rises with leverage
questions.explanation: The (1-t) factor on the debt side creates a tax wedge. Higher D/V → more weight on the tax-shielded debt rate → lower WACC.

questions.prompt: Pushed to its logical conclusion, MM-with-taxes implies firms should be:
choices: 100% debt-financed (because the tax shield never stops adding value)
questions.explanation: MM-with-taxes alone says go to 100% debt. Empirically firms don't, because distress costs (next lesson) push back.
